items.title: Definition and classification
items.contentHtml:
Definition and classification acute/chronic

  • Dacryocystitis is the inflammation of the lacrimal sac, secondary to obstruction of the nasolacrimal duct.
  • It is classified as acute (sudden onset, pain, erythema, edema in the medial canthus, fever) and chronic (epiphora, mucous secretion, lacrimal regurgitation, without acute inflammatory signs).
  • The acute form can progress to lacrimal sac abscess or orbital cellulitis; chronic is the most common in adults.

items.foco: Most common obstructive etiology in adults
items.title: Obstructive etiology in adults
items.contentHtml:
Most common obstructive etiology in adults

  • The most common cause of obstruction of the nasolacrimal duct in adults is idiopathic (involutive), associated with fibrosis and progressive narrowing.
  • Other causes: naso-orbital trauma, previous sinus surgery, neoplasias (papilloma, carcinoma paranasal sinus), and drugs such as eye drops with timolol or pilocarpine.
  • In postmenopausal women it is more common due to hormonal changes that affect the nasolacrimal mucosa.

items.foco: Main causal agent in acute dacryocystitis
items.title: Main causal agent
items.contentHtml:
Main causative agent in acute dacryocystitis

  • The most common agent in acute dacryocystitis is Staphylococcus aureus (including methicillin-resistant strains).
  • Secondly: Streptococcus pneumoniae and Haemophilus influenzae (especially in children).
  • In chronic forms Gram-negatives such as Pseudomonas aeruginosa and anaerobes predominate; Therefore, the antibiogram is crucial before prolonged treatment.

items.foco: Sign of regurgitation of the lacrimal sac
items.title: Regurgitation sign
items.contentHtml:
Lacrimal sac regurgitation sign

  • The lacrimal sac regurgitation sign (or Rosenbach sign) is obtained by pressing the medial canthus and observing the release of mucopurulent material through the lacrimal punctum.
  • It is positive in distal obstruction of the nasolacrimal duct with a distended lacrimal sac (dacryocystocele or chronic dacryocystitis).
  • Negative in proximal obstruction (canaliculitis) or in the absence of contents in the sac.

items.title: classical triad
items.contentHtml:
Classic triad of chronic dacryocystitis

  • The classic triad of chronic dacryocystitis includes: epiphora (persistent tearing), mucous or mucopurulent discharge when pressing the lacrimal sac, and positive regurgitation of the lacrimal sac.
  • It can be associated with conjunctivitis. chronic and blepharitis due to reflux of infectious content.
  • It usually does not present pain or fever, unlike the acute form.

items.title: Initial antibiotic treatment
items.contentHtml:
Initial treatment with oral antibiotics

  • In acute dacryocystitis amoxicillin/clavulanic acid 875/125 mg every 12 hours for 7-10 days is started, or cephalexin 500 mg every 6 hours if allergic to penicillin.
  • In cases of suspected MRSA (institutionalized patient, recurrent infection) use trimethoprim/sulfamethoxazole or clindamycin.
  • It is complemented with warm compresses and massage of the lacrimal sac; if there is an abscess, surgical drainage.

items.title: Dacryocystorhinostomy
items.contentHtml:
Dacryocystorhinostomy as definitive treatment

  • The dacryocystorhinostomy (DCR) is the definitive surgical treatment for obstruction of the nasolacrimal duct that does not respond to medical management.
  • It can be performed by external (skin incision) or endonasal (with an endoscope), creating a fistula between the lacrimal sac. and the nasal cavity.
  • Indications: chronic recurrent dacryocystitis, recurrent abscess, failure of probing or dacryocystitis in older adults.

items.title: Complication: cellulitis
items.contentHtml:
Complication: preseptal or orbital cellulitis

  • Acute dacryocystitis can be complicated by preseptal cellulitis (eyelid edema without orbital involvement) or orbital cellulitis (proptosis, ophthalmoplegia, decreased visual acuity).
  • Orbital cellulitis is an emergency: it requires computed tomography of the orbits and intravenous treatment with vancomycin plus ceftriaxone.
  • Risk factors: diabetes, immunosuppression, delay in antibiotic treatment.

items.title: Differential diagnosis: canaliculitis
items.contentHtml:
Differential diagnosis with canaliculitis

  • Canaliculitis is inflammation of the lacrimal canaliculum, often caused by Actinomyces israelii or Streptococcus, and is differentiated from dacryocystitis by presenting erythematous and bulging lacrimal punctum with purulent discharge when pressing the canaliculus.
  • In dacryocystitis the lacrimal punctum is normal and the regurgitation comes from the lacrimal sac.
  • Canaliculitis does not respond to DCR; requires canaliculotomy and extraction of concretions (dacryoliths).

items.title: Risk factors in neonates
items.contentHtml:
Risk factors in newborns

  • In newborns, dacryocystitis is associated with congenital obstruction of the nasolacrimal duct due to persistent Hasner's membrane.
  • Risk factors: prematurity, traumatic birth, craniofacial syndromes (trisomy 21, cleft palate) and maternal Chlamydia trachomatis infection.
  • Initial treatment is Crigler massage and catheterization; the majority resolve spontaneously at one year of age.

items.foco: Culture and antibiogram in recurrent cases
items.title: Culture and antibiogram
items.contentHtml:
Culture and antibiogram in recurrent cases

  • culture and antibiogram of tear secretion is indicated in recurrent dacryocystitis, therapeutic failure, or nosocomial infection.
  • The sample is obtained by swabbing the lacrimal sac after pressing the medial canthus, avoiding contamination with flora conjunctival.
  • The pathogens most isolated in recurrences are Pseudomonas aeruginosa, MRSA and anaerobes; The antibiogram guides targeted oral or intravenous therapy.

items.title: Pre-surgical dacryocystography
items.contentHtml:
Indication for presurgical dacryocystography

  • dacryocystography (with water-soluble contrast) is indicated before dacryocystorhinostomy to evaluate the anatomy of the lacrimal sac, locate the exact site of obstruction and rule out intraluminal masses (dacryoliths, tumors).
  • It is performed in cases of chronic dacryocystitis. recurrent , suspected neoplasia or failure of previous DCR.
  • Computed tomography with contrast is an alternative when orbital or sinus extension is suspected.
